results.question: How does algorithmic bias manifest in healthcare AI, and what mitigation strategies have evidence?
results.answer: safety reviews and that all training data be approved by healthcare authorities. results.expected_answer: Bias was documented in 42% of 234 evaluated systems. Types include racial bias (68 studies, e.g., Optum algorithm underestimating Black patient needs), gender bias (31 studies, cardiovascular risk under-prediction in women), age bias (19 studies), and socioeconomic bias (23 studies). Evidence-based mitigations: prospective bias audits (NIST framework), stratified performance reporting, adversarial debiasing, causal modeling, and diverse development teams (30%+ underrepresented members → 50% fewer bias incidents).

results.question: What AI approaches are being used to address the antibiotic resistance crisis?
results.answer: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) kills an estimated 1.27 million people annually and is projected to cause 10 million deaths per year by 2050. [Citation: DOC-052] Hospital-acquired infections (HAIs) affect 1 in 31 hospital patients in the US, causing approximately 99,000 deaths and $33 billion in excess costs annually. [Citation: DOC-053]
results.expected_answer: MIT researchers used a graph neural network on 39,000 compounds to discover halicin (effective against pan-resistant A. baumannii), then a molecular transformer on 12M compounds to discover abaucin (species-specific). Phare Bio used diffusion models to generate novel molecules, finding 8 compounds active against MRSA with 3 entering preclinical development. AI advantage: can optimize for specific properties (narrow-spectrum, resistance to known resistance mechanisms). Challenge: development still takes 5-10 years and $300M-$1B per drug.
